quiz.question: The port specialised in handling goods shipped in bulk. What does 'in bulk' mean?
options: In large quantities without individual packaging
In containers designed to prevent damage in transit
Through specialist freight companies rather than general carriers
Using standardised cargo dimensions for efficient loading
quiz.answer: In large quantities without individual packaging
quiz.explanation:
In bulk means in large unpackaged quantities.
• bulk = mass; volume; large quantity
• From Old Norse: 'bulki' — a heap of cargo
• 'Buy in bulk', 'bulk order', 'bulk carrier' — large unpackaged quantities

quiz.question: The rusting hulk of an old freighter sat on the beach where it had run aground decades ago. What is a hulk?
options: The body of an old or wrecked ship
A vessel too damaged to be repaired and returned to service
Any large vessel that had been taken out of commercial use
The remains of a ship that had sunk close to shore
quiz.answer: The body of an old or wrecked ship
quiz.explanation:
A hulk is the shell of an old or wrecked ship — or anything large and unwieldy.
• hulk = the hull of an old ship; a big, clumsy person or thing
• From Old English: 'hulc' — a heavy ship
• 'A rusting hulk', 'the Incredible Hulk' — large, solid, and somewhat inert

quiz.question: The travelling mountebank sold bottles of miracle tonic from the back of a cart. What is a mountebank?
options: A charlatan who deceives people with false claims
A travelling salesman who sold medicines of doubtful value
A performer who combined entertainment with selling
A dealer in counterfeit goods who moved from town to town
quiz.answer: A charlatan who deceives people with false claims
quiz.explanation:
A mountebank is a charlatan — someone who deceives through false expertise.
• mountebank = a person who deceives; a charlatan
• From Italian: 'montambanco' — one who mounts a bench (to speak to a crowd)
• 'A travelling mountebank', 'political mountebank' — a fraud who performs

quiz.question: The practice of spanking children as discipline is now banned in many countries. What does 'spank' mean?
options: To slap on the buttocks as punishment
To use physical force as a form of correction
To discipline through a controlled physical intervention
To apply a punishment that leaves no lasting physical mark
quiz.answer: To slap on the buttocks as punishment
quiz.explanation:
To spank means to slap on the buttocks — a form of physical punishment.
• spank = to slap on the buttocks; to move briskly
• Probably imitative
• 'Brand new' is unrelated; 'spanking new' = very new; 'spank' = slap

quiz.question: They treated themselves to a night in a swank hotel to celebrate. What does 'swank' mean?
options: Impressively luxurious — fashionably smart
More expensive than their usual choice of accommodation
Well-reviewed enough to justify the price
Known for the quality of its service and its location
quiz.answer: Impressively luxurious — fashionably smart
quiz.explanation:
Swank means fashionably smart and luxurious — impressively upmarket.
• swank = ostentatiously smart; to show off
• Origin uncertain
• 'A swank restaurant', 'swanky hotel' — luxurious and fashionable

quiz.question: The child shrank back in fear as the dog approached. What does 'shrink' mean here?
options: To draw back — to retreat from something frightening
To react physically to an unexpected stimulus
To show fear through body language before speaking
To step away instinctively from a threatening animal, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To draw back — to retreat from something frightening
quiz.explanation:
To shrink (from) means to draw back — to recoil in fear or reluctance.
• shrink = to draw back; to become smaller
• From Old English: 'scrincan' — to contract
• 'Shrink from responsibility', 'shrink in horror' — recoiling or withdrawing

quiz.question: The regulator had long winked at the practice, despite its illegality. What does 'wink at' mean?
options: To deliberately overlook — to turn a blind eye to
To approve of something that technically required scrutiny
To avoid pursuing a violation that was widely known
To be aware of an infraction without taking formal action
quiz.answer: To deliberately overlook — to turn a blind eye to
quiz.explanation:
To wink at means to pretend not to see — to deliberately overlook.
• wink at = to deliberately ignore a fault or transgression
• From 'wink' — to close the eye briefly
• 'Wink at corruption', 'wink at a violation' — complicit non-enforcement

quiz.question: He cut himself a hunk of bread and ate it with cheese. What is a hunk?
options: A large, thick piece cut from something
A portion that was more than enough for one sitting
A rough cut of something that had not been neatly sliced
A slice taken with a large knife from an uncut loaf
quiz.answer: A large, thick piece cut from something
quiz.explanation:
A hunk is a large thick piece — cut roughly from a larger whole.
• hunk = a large piece cut or broken off
• From Flemish: 'hunke'
• 'A hunk of cheese', 'a hunk of meat' — a substantial, rough piece

quiz.question: A chunk of ice broke off the glacier and crashed into the sea. What is a chunk?
options: A thick, solid piece of something
A large fragment produced by a fracture in the ice
A portion of a larger structure that had broken away
An irregular mass that had separated from the main body
quiz.answer: A thick, solid piece of something
quiz.explanation:
A chunk is a thick solid piece — a substantial portion of something.
• chunk = a thick, solid piece
• Variant of 'chuck' — a block
• 'A chunk of time', 'a chunk of rock' — a substantial portion

quiz.question: A skunk sprays a foul-smelling liquid when it feels threatened. What is a skunk?
options: A North American mammal that defends itself with a noxious spray
A nocturnal animal known for its distinctive black and white markings
A small predator that uses chemical defence against larger animals
A marsupial found across North America with a distinctive scent gland
quiz.answer: A North American mammal that defends itself with a noxious spray
quiz.explanation:
A skunk is a black-and-white North American mammal that sprays a foul liquid.
• skunk = Mephitis mephitis; a mammal with a powerful defensive scent
• From Algonquian: 'segankw'
• 'Smells like a skunk', 'skunked' — also: to defeat completely

quiz.question: He flunked three exams before finally passing on the fourth attempt. What does 'flunk' mean?
options: To fail — to not reach the required standard
To sit an exam without adequate preparation
To submit work of insufficient quality
To achieve a score below what he was capable of
quiz.answer: To fail — to not reach the required standard
quiz.explanation:
To flunk means to fail — to not meet the required standard in a test.
• flunk = to fail an exam; to give a failing grade
• Origin uncertain; possibly from 'flinch'
• 'Flunk out', 'flunked the test' — failing to pass

quiz.question: The child's cheeks were so full of food he looked like a chipmunk. What is the image conveying?
options: Cheeks puffed out with stored food — excessively full
A resemblance to the animal's colouring
An expression of pleasure at what was being eaten, as the evidence showed
A habit of hoarding food that ran in the family
quiz.answer: Cheeks puffed out with stored food — excessively full
quiz.explanation:
A chipmunk stores food in its cheek pouches — the image describes puffed-out cheeks.
• chipmunk = a small North American rodent that stores food in its cheeks
• From Ojibwe: 'ajidamoo'
• 'Chipmunk cheeks' = cheeks bulging with stored food

quiz.question: The young player showed real spunk, refusing to quit despite the score. What is spunk?
options: Courage and determination — gutsy spirit
A talent for the game that showed despite the difficulties
The willingness to continue when less motivated players might stop
A competitive instinct that set her apart from her teammates
quiz.answer: Courage and determination — gutsy spirit
quiz.explanation:
Spunk means courage and grit — spirited determination.
• spunk = courage; spirit; guts
• From Scottish Gaelic: 'spong' — tinder (a spark of fire)
• 'Show some spunk', 'full of spunk' — determined, gutsy

quiz.question: The auditor had overlooked a crucial detail that changed the whole picture. What does 'overlook' mean?
options: To fail to notice or consider — to miss
To decide a detail was not important enough to include
To see something but not understand its significance
To omit a finding from the final report by mistake
quiz.answer: To fail to notice or consider — to miss
quiz.explanation:
To overlook means to miss — to fail to notice or consider something.
• overlook = to fail to notice; to ignore deliberately; also to look out over
• From 'over' + 'look'
• 'Overlooked an error', 'overlook a fault' — failing to see or choosing to ignore

quiz.question: The chairman brooked no dissent — any objection was met with immediate dismissal. What does 'brook' mean?
options: To tolerate — to accept without objection
To allow in an organization where discipline was paramount
To hear and consider before reaching a final decision
To manage in a way that prevented it from becoming disruptive
quiz.answer: To tolerate — to accept without objection
quiz.explanation:
To brook means to tolerate — used almost always in the negative.
• brook = to tolerate; to endure
• From Old English: 'brucan' — to use
• 'Brook no nonsense', 'brook no opposition' — not tolerating

quiz.question: The sergeant barked orders at the recruits without pausing for breath. What does 'bark' mean?
options: To shout commands in a sharp, abrupt manner
To issue orders in a way that left no room for discussion
To speak too loudly for an indoor training environment
To communicate in a style that intimidated new recruits
quiz.answer: To shout commands in a sharp, abrupt manner
quiz.explanation:
To bark means to shout sharply — to give orders in a loud, abrupt voice.
• bark = to make a sharp abrupt sound; to shout commands
• From Old English: 'beorcan'
• 'Bark orders', 'bark at someone' — sharp, abrupt shouting

quiz.question: Passengers were asked to debark in an orderly fashion. What does 'debark' mean?
options: To disembark — to leave a ship or aircraft
To exit a vessel after it has docked
To be guided off a ship by crew members
To proceed through passport control after a sea voyage
quiz.answer: To disembark — to leave a ship or aircraft
quiz.explanation:
To debark means to disembark — to leave a ship, aircraft, or vehicle.
• debark = to disembark; to leave a vessel
• From French: 'débarquer' — to unload from a ship
• 'Debark passengers', 'debarking procedure' — leaving a vessel

quiz.question: The hallmark of her work was meticulous attention to every detail. What is a hallmark?
options: A distinctive feature or quality that identifies something
The quality that made her work recognisable to those who knew it
The standard she had established and consistently maintained
A characteristic so consistent it had become her professional identity
quiz.answer: A distinctive feature or quality that identifies something
quiz.explanation:
A hallmark is a distinguishing characteristic — a quality that identifies something.
• hallmark = a distinctive feature; a mark guaranteeing quality
• From Goldsmiths' Hall in London, where gold and silver was tested
• 'The hallmark of excellence', 'hallmark of the brand' — the identifying quality

quiz.question: The discovery sparked widespread interest in the ancient civilisation. What does 'spark' mean here?
options: To ignite or trigger — to set something in motion
To generate attention in communities that had not previously engaged
To create a level of interest that sustained further investigation
To produce a reaction that surprised even those who made the discovery
quiz.answer: To ignite or trigger — to set something in motion
quiz.explanation:
To spark means to ignite or trigger — to set off a reaction or process.
• spark = to trigger; to ignite
• From Old English: 'spearca' — a spark
• 'Spark debate', 'spark a revolution' — triggering a reaction

quiz.question: The stark contrast between the two neighbourhoods was impossible to ignore. What does 'stark' mean?
options: Sharply clear and extreme — without softening or nuance
Noticed by everyone who passed through both areas
Visible in the architecture as much as in the social statistics
More pronounced than it had been in the previous decade
quiz.answer: Sharply clear and extreme — without softening or nuance
quiz.explanation:
Stark means complete and harsh — starkly clear and extreme.
• stark = severe; bare; complete
• From Old English: 'stearc' — stiff
• 'Stark contrast', 'stark raving mad', 'stark naked' — unmitigated, bare

quiz.question: The car came to a sudden jerk as the brakes locked up. What is a jerk?
options: A sharp, abrupt movement or pull
The physical sensation produced by emergency braking
A movement violent enough to cause discomfort to passengers
A rapid deceleration felt throughout the vehicle
quiz.answer: A sharp, abrupt movement or pull
quiz.explanation:
A jerk is a sudden sharp movement — an abrupt, unsmooth pull or thrust.
• jerk = a sudden, sharp movement; to move with a jerk
• Origin uncertain; probably imitative
• 'A jerk of the head', 'jerk the wheel' — sudden, abrupt movement

quiz.question: He was known for shirking the responsibilities that came with the role. What does 'shirk' mean?
options: To avoid doing — to evade duties
To accept a role while declining the work it required
To delegate tasks that were expected to be handled personally
To miss obligations that others had always fulfilled
quiz.answer: To avoid doing — to evade duties
quiz.explanation:
To shirk means to evade — to avoid responsibilities one should fulfil.
• shirk = to avoid duty or work
• From German: 'schurke' — a scoundrel
• 'Shirk duty', 'a shirker' — evading what is expected

quiz.question: By a quirk of fate, the two rivals ended up seated next to each other. What is a quirk?
options: An unexpected twist — a strange or unusual occurrence
A coincidence that nobody would have predicted
An arrangement produced by chance rather than intention
A situation that produced an outcome nobody had foreseen
quiz.answer: An unexpected twist — a strange or unusual occurrence
quiz.explanation:
A quirk is a peculiar twist — an unexpected or unusual feature.
• quirk = a peculiarity; an unexpected twist
• Origin uncertain
• 'A quirk of fate', 'a personal quirk' — an odd feature or unexpected turn

quiz.question: The old building's balcony was decorated with delicate iron fretwork. What is fretwork?
options: Decorative ornamental work with interlaced or perforated patterns
Ironwork designed to strengthen as well as decorate a structure
The kind of detailed ironwork associated with Victorian architecture
Decorative panels installed between the railings of a balcony
quiz.answer: Decorative ornamental work with interlaced or perforated patterns
quiz.explanation:
Fretwork is ornamental work with interlaced or openwork patterns.
• fretwork = decorative work with a pattern of interlaced lines or holes
• From 'fret' — an ornamental pattern
• 'Iron fretwork', 'wood fretwork' — decorative openwork patterns

quiz.question: The obelisk at the city's centre had been brought from Egypt two centuries earlier. What is an obelisk?
options: A tall, tapering four-sided stone monument with a pyramidal top
An ancient Egyptian column used to mark important sites
A monumental stone column erected as a public memorial
A tall stone structure whose purpose was primarily astronomical, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A tall, tapering four-sided stone monument with a pyramidal top
quiz.explanation:
An obelisk is a tall tapering stone pillar — four-sided with a pointed top.
• obelisk = a tall stone pillar with a pyramidal apex
• From Greek: 'obeliskos' — a small spit
• 'Cleopatra's Needle', 'Washington Monument' — obelisks

quiz.question: An asterisk marks the exception listed in the footnote. What is an asterisk?
options: The star-shaped symbol (*) used to mark a note or qualification
A punctuation mark used to direct the reader to additional detail
A typographical device that indicates a reference in the text
A symbol used in printing to draw attention to an exception
quiz.answer: The star-shaped symbol (*) used to mark a note or qualification
quiz.explanation:
An asterisk is the star symbol (*) — used to indicate a footnote or qualification.
• asterisk = the symbol (*) used to mark a note or reference
• From Greek: 'asteriskos' — a little star
• 'Asterisk a term', 'with an asterisk' — marked with a caveat

quiz.question: She bought her tickets from a kiosk near the theatre entrance. What is a kiosk?
options: A small open-fronted booth or structure for selling things
A temporary structure set up near an entrance to a venue
A counter staffed by a single person serving customers
A small building providing a service at a public location, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A small open-fronted booth or structure for selling things
quiz.explanation:
A kiosk is a small booth — an open-fronted structure for selling goods or services.
• kiosk = a small booth or stall
• From Turkish: 'köşk' — a pavilion
• 'Ticket kiosk', 'newspaper kiosk', 'self-service kiosk' — small booths

quiz.question: The husks were stripped from the corn before it was cooked. What is a husk?
options: The dry outer covering of a seed or fruit
The leaves that wrap around an ear of corn before harvest
The papery outer layer of grain that is removed in milling
The protective layer that keeps a seed moist before germination
quiz.answer: The dry outer covering of a seed or fruit
quiz.explanation:
A husk is the dry outer covering of a seed or grain.
• husk = the dry outer covering of seeds and fruits
• From Middle Dutch: 'huuskijn' — a little house
• 'Corn husk', 'rice husk' — the outer covering

quiz.question: The trade in elephant tusks is banned under international law. What is a tusk?
options: A long projecting tooth — especially of an elephant or walrus
The ivory that forms the longest teeth of certain mammals, as the evidence showed
A modified canine tooth that grows outside the jaw
The extended tooth of a large mammal used for digging
quiz.answer: A long projecting tooth — especially of an elephant or walrus
quiz.explanation:
A tusk is a long projecting tooth — particularly of elephants, walruses, and boars.
• tusk = a long pointed tooth projecting from the closed mouth
• From Old English: 'tux' — tooth
• 'Elephant tusk', 'ivory tusk' — the elongated tooth used for ivory

quiz.question: The salesman hawked his wares loudly at every market he attended. What does 'hawk' mean?
options: To sell aggressively — to peddle goods by calling out
To promote a product using techniques that attracted a crowd
To sell directly to customers rather than through retailers
To offer goods at lower prices than established competitors
quiz.answer: To sell aggressively — to peddle goods by calling out
quiz.explanation:
To hawk means to sell aggressively — to peddle goods by loudly calling.
• hawk = to sell by calling out; to peddle
• From Middle Low German: 'höker' — a retailer
• 'Hawk newspapers', 'hawking goods' — selling by loud promotion

quiz.question: A small cabal within the party was working to unseat the leader. What is a cabal?
options: A small group united in a secret plot
A faction that worked to undermine the existing leadership
A political group acting outside normal party processes
A group of conspirators meeting without official sanction
quiz.answer: A small group united in a secret plot
quiz.explanation:
A cabal is a secret conspiratorial group — a small faction plotting together.
• cabal = a small group engaged in secret intrigue
• From Hebrew: 'qabbalah' — mystical interpretation
• 'A political cabal', 'a cabal of plotters' — a secret conspiring group

quiz.question: She filed a complaint about the verbal abuse she had received from a colleague. What is verbal abuse?
options: The use of words to hurt, threaten, or demean
Language that violated the organisation's code of conduct
Insults severe enough to constitute a formal disciplinary matter
Language directed at a colleague that created a hostile environment
quiz.answer: The use of words to hurt, threaten, or demean
quiz.explanation:
Verbal abuse is harmful language — using words to hurt, threaten, or demean.
• verbal = relating to words
• abuse = to use wrongly; to treat badly
• 'Verbal abuse', 'verbal agreement', 'verbal warning' — all involving words

quiz.question: Her methodical approach to the problem produced clear, reproducible results. What does 'methodical' mean?
options: Done in a systematic, orderly way
Based on a method that others in the field could replicate
Thorough in a way that minimised the risk of error
Structured around a clear sequence that produced consistent results
quiz.answer: Done in a systematic, orderly way
quiz.explanation:
Methodical means systematic — proceeding in an orderly, disciplined way.
• methodical = acting according to a clear, systematic method
• From Greek: 'methodos' — a pursuit
• 'Methodical research', 'methodical thinker' — careful and systematic

quiz.question: The finding was published in a leading academic periodical. What is a periodical?
options: A publication issued at regular intervals — a journal or magazine
A journal publishing peer-reviewed research in a given field
A publication whose editorial standards are set by its discipline
A scholarly outlet that accepts contributions from researchers worldwide
quiz.answer: A publication issued at regular intervals — a journal or magazine
quiz.explanation:
A periodical is a publication issued regularly — at fixed intervals.
• periodical = a magazine or journal published at regular intervals
• From Greek: 'periodos' — a cycle
• 'Academic periodical', 'trade periodical' — published at regular intervals

quiz.question: He adopted a pontifical tone that suggested he alone understood the issue. What does 'pontifical' mean?
options: Self-importantly authoritative — pompously certain
Formal in a way that underlined the seriousness of the subject
Academic in a way that some found difficult to follow
Authoritative because of his long experience in the field
quiz.answer: Self-importantly authoritative — pompously certain
quiz.explanation:
Pontifical means pompously authoritative — speaking as if with supreme authority.
• pontifical = relating to a pontiff; also: self-importantly authoritative
• From Latin: 'pontifex' — high priest
• 'A pontifical manner' = speaking as if the Pope; pompously authoritative

quiz.question: He was described by his colleagues as a pathological liar who could not stop inventing stories. What does 'pathological' mean?
options: Compulsive and beyond normal rational control
Dishonest to a degree that could no longer be excused
Habitual in a way that had damaged his professional standing
So deeply ingrained that it defined his character entirely
quiz.answer: Compulsive and beyond normal rational control
quiz.explanation:
Pathological means compulsive — driven by a psychological condition beyond control.
• pathological = caused by or relating to disease; compulsive
• From Greek: 'pathos' (suffering) + 'logos' (study)
• 'Pathological liar', 'pathological jealousy' — beyond normal rational behaviour

quiz.question: Fear triggers a physiological response — the heart rate rises and muscles tense. What does 'physiological' mean?
options: Relating to the physical functions of a living organism
Involving bodily processes rather than psychological ones
Automatic in a way that bypasses conscious decision-making
Measurable through physical indicators such as heart rate
quiz.answer: Relating to the physical functions of a living organism
quiz.explanation:
Physiological means relating to physiology — the functions of the body.
• physiological = relating to the normal functions of living organisms
• From Greek: 'physis' (nature) + 'logos' (study)
• 'Physiological response', 'physiological needs' — the body's functions

quiz.question: The argument was tautological — it simply restated its premise as its conclusion. What does 'tautological' mean?
options: Saying the same thing twice in different words — circular
Poorly constructed in a way that undermined its own logic
Beginning and ending at the same point without adding anything
Repeating a point in terms that sounded different but were identical
quiz.answer: Saying the same thing twice in different words — circular
quiz.explanation:
Tautological means needlessly repetitive — saying the same thing twice.
• tautological = involving tautology; circular and redundant
• From Greek: 'tauto' (the same) + 'logos' (word)
• 'A tautological statement', 'tautology' — adding no new information

quiz.question: The letter was marred by a typographical error on the very first line. What is a typographical error?
options: A mistake in the printing or typing of text
An error caused by pressing the wrong key
A spelling mistake introduced during the word-processing stage
An error that a proofreader should have caught
quiz.answer: A mistake in the printing or typing of text
quiz.explanation:
A typographical error is a typo — a mistake made in typing or printing text.
• typographical = relating to printing or type
• From Greek: 'typos' (impression) + 'graphein' (to write)
• 'Typographical error', 'typo' — a printing or typing mistake

quiz.question: Economists described the recession as a cyclical downturn rather than a structural crisis. What does 'cyclical' mean?
options: Recurring in regular cycles — following a repeating pattern
Temporary in a way that distinguished it from deeper problems
Predictable enough to be modelled using standard economic tools
Related to the normal fluctuations of a market economy
quiz.answer: Recurring in regular cycles — following a repeating pattern
quiz.explanation:
Cyclical means recurring in cycles — following a regular repeating pattern.
• cyclical = occurring in cycles; regularly repeated
• From Greek: 'kyklos' — a circle
• 'Cyclical recession', 'cyclical pattern' — repeating rhythmically

quiz.question: The villain's diabolical scheme nearly succeeded before it was uncovered. What does 'diabolical' mean?
options: Wicked and fiendishly clever — of the devil
Illegal in a way that would lead to serious consequences
Elaborate enough to have taken months of planning
Designed to harm people who had done nothing to deserve it
quiz.answer: Wicked and fiendishly clever — of the devil
quiz.explanation:
Diabolical means fiendishly wicked — devilish in character.
• diabolical = of the devil; extremely bad or wicked
• From Greek: 'diabolos' — the devil
• 'Diabolical scheme', 'diabolical weather' — devilish (or informally: very bad)

quiz.question: The polemical essay attacked the government's education policy with fierce directness. What does 'polemical' mean?
options: Strongly argumentative — intended to attack a position
Published without the moderation expected in academic writing
Critical in a way that made its political allegiances clear
Written in a style that prioritised impact over balance
quiz.answer: Strongly argumentative — intended to attack a position
quiz.explanation:
Polemical means contentiously argumentative — written to attack and provoke.
• polemical = of or involving strongly contentious argument
• From Greek: 'polemos' — war
• 'Polemical tone', 'a polemic against austerity' — combative argument

quiz.question: Sedentary living is inimical to long-term health. What does 'inimical' mean?
options: Harmful and opposed to — tending to obstruct or damage
Associated in studies with higher rates of chronic disease
Likely to produce negative health outcomes over the long term
Not compatible with the lifestyle changes doctors recommend
quiz.answer: Harmful and opposed to — tending to obstruct or damage
quiz.explanation:
Inimical means hostile and harmful — tending to work against something.
• inimical = harmful; hostile; acting against
• From Latin: 'inimicus' — an enemy
• 'Inimical to democracy', 'inimical to health' — working against

quiz.question: The small economical car was popular with drivers who wanted low running costs. What does 'economical' mean?
options: Using resources efficiently — giving good value for money spent
Priced attractively for buyers on a limited budget
Small enough to qualify for the lowest road tax band
Cheaper to insure than equivalent vehicles in the same class, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Using resources efficiently — giving good value for money spent
quiz.explanation:
Economical means efficient with resources — giving good value relative to cost.
• economical = avoiding waste; efficient with resources
• From Greek: 'oikonomia' — household management
• 'Economical car', 'economical with the truth' — efficient or sparing

quiz.question: The asking price for the property was astronomical — far beyond what most buyers could consider. What does 'astronomical' mean?
options: Immensely large — on a scale that defies normal comprehension
Higher than any comparable property in the same area
Set at a level that reflected the seller's unrealistic expectations
Beyond what any buyer without specialist financing could afford
quiz.answer: Immensely large — on a scale that defies normal comprehension
quiz.explanation:
Astronomical means immensely large — of a scale comparable to distances in space.
• astronomical = of or relating to astronomy; extremely large
• From Greek: 'astronomia' — star arrangement
• 'Astronomical price', 'astronomical figures' — beyond normal scale

quiz.question: The medical students studied the anatomical model to understand the structure of the heart. What does 'anatomical' mean?
options: Relating to the structure of a body or its parts
Designed to replicate the physical form of an organ
Used in medical training to replace the use of specimens
Representing the internal arrangement of the cardiovascular system
quiz.answer: Relating to the structure of a body or its parts
quiz.explanation:
Anatomical means relating to anatomy — the structure of the body.
• anatomical = relating to the structure of the body
• From Greek: 'ana-' (up) + 'temnein' (to cut)
• 'Anatomical model', 'anatomical variation' — the structure of the body

quiz.question: The aircraft returned to the airport due to a mechanical failure. What does 'mechanical' mean?
options: Relating to machinery and its physical workings
Caused by a problem with the engines or moving parts
Diagnosed by engineers rather than by software systems
Requiring physical repair rather than a software update
quiz.answer: Relating to machinery and its physical workings
quiz.explanation:
Mechanical means relating to machinery — involving physical mechanisms.
• mechanical = relating to machines; done without thought
• From Greek: 'mechane' — a machine
• 'Mechanical failure', 'mechanical response' — machine-related or automatic

quiz.question: The ecumenical council brought together representatives of many Christian denominations. What does 'ecumenical' mean?
options: Representing the whole Christian Church across denominations
Including the largest and most influential Christian traditions
Seeking dialogue between churches that had been separated
Convened to address issues affecting all Christian communities
quiz.answer: Representing the whole Christian Church across denominations
quiz.explanation:
Ecumenical means representing the worldwide Christian Church — across denominations.
• ecumenical = representing the whole of the Christian Church; seeking unity
• From Greek: 'oikoumene' — the inhabited world
• 'Ecumenical movement', 'ecumenical dialogue' — Christian unity across churches

quiz.question: The play is now considered a canonical text in English literature. What does 'canonical' mean?
options: Officially recognised as authoritative or standard
Studied in schools and universities as part of the curriculum
Acclaimed by critics and readers over many generations
Included in every anthology of significant literary works
quiz.answer: Officially recognised as authoritative or standard
quiz.explanation:
Canonical means belonging to the accepted body of authoritative works.
• canonical = included in the official accepted list; authoritative
• From Greek: 'kanon' — a rule or standard
• 'The literary canon', 'canonical text' — the authoritative, accepted body of works

quiz.question: The theory must be tested against empirical evidence before it is accepted. What does 'empirical' mean?
options: Based on observation and experiment rather than theory alone
Gathered through controlled experiments that can be repeated, as the evidence showed
Accepted by the scientific community after peer review
Strong enough to be used as the basis of a scientific claim
quiz.answer: Based on observation and experiment rather than theory alone
quiz.explanation:
Empirical means based on observation — derived from experiment and evidence.
• empirical = based on observation and experience
• From Greek: 'empeiria' — experience
• 'Empirical evidence', 'empirical research' — tested against reality

quiz.question: The poem followed a strict metrical pattern throughout. What does 'metrical' mean?
options: Relating to the measured rhythm of verse
Structured according to the rules of classical prosody
Adhering to a line length consistent with the chosen form
Organised so that stressed and unstressed syllables alternated
quiz.answer: Relating to the measured rhythm of verse
quiz.explanation:
Metrical means relating to metre — the rhythmic structure of verse.
• metrical = of or relating to poetic metre
• From Greek: 'metron' — a measure
• 'Metrical pattern', 'metrical feet' — the rhythmic units of verse

quiz.question: His lackadaisical attitude to deadlines made him unpopular with colleagues. What does 'lackadaisical' mean?
options: Lacking energy and enthusiasm — carelessly casual
Unreliable in a way that was difficult to address formally
Unable to maintain the focus that the work required
Producing work that was acceptable but never outstanding
quiz.answer: Lacking energy and enthusiasm — carelessly casual
quiz.explanation:
Lackadaisical means lacking energy and care — casually indifferent.
• lackadaisical = lacking enthusiasm and determination; carelessly lazy
• From 'lackaday' — an exclamation of regret
• 'Lackadaisical effort', 'lackadaisical approach' — without energy or care

quiz.question: The architect came up with a whimsical idea to build a treehouse for the boardroom. What does 'whimsical' mean?
options: Playfully fanciful — charmingly unusual and imaginative
Impractical but appealing in a way that charmed the client
Creative in a way that required imagination to appreciate
Unusual enough to generate publicity for the firm
quiz.answer: Playfully fanciful — charmingly unusual and imaginative
quiz.explanation:
Whimsical means playfully fanciful — charming in its unusual imagination.
• whimsical = playfully quaint; given to whims
• From 'whim' — a sudden fancy
• 'A whimsical idea', 'whimsical humor' — imaginatively and playfully unusual

quiz.question: His answer was so nonsensical that the interviewer moved quickly on. What does 'nonsensical' mean?
options: Making no sense — absurd and incomprehensible
Irrelevant to the question that had been asked
Confused in a way that suggested he had not prepared
So vague it was impossible to evaluate as right or wrong
quiz.answer: Making no sense — absurd and incomprehensible
quiz.explanation:
Nonsensical means making no sense — absurd or meaningless.
• nonsensical = having no sense; absurd
• From 'non-' + 'sense'
• 'Nonsensical argument', 'nonsensical claim' — without meaning or logic

quiz.question: Questions about consciousness and free will are among the great metaphysical puzzles. What does 'metaphysical' mean?
options: Relating to the fundamental nature of reality and existence
Beyond the reach of science and requiring philosophical analysis
Concerning questions that have occupied thinkers for millennia
Related to the abstract rather than the material world
quiz.answer: Relating to the fundamental nature of reality and existence
quiz.explanation:
Metaphysical means concerning fundamental reality — beyond what physics can explain.
• metaphysical = relating to the nature of reality, existence, and being
• From Greek: 'meta' (after) + 'physika' (physics)
• 'Metaphysical question', 'metaphysical poetry' — concerning ultimate reality

quiz.question: The entries in the index were arranged in alphabetical order. What does 'alphabetical' mean?
options: Arranged by the letters of the alphabet in sequence
Organised using a standard system of categorisation
Presented in a way that made individual items easy to locate
Listed from the first entry to the last without grouping by category
quiz.answer: Arranged by the letters of the alphabet in sequence
quiz.explanation:
Alphabetical means arranged by the letters of the alphabet in order.
• alphabetical = in the order of the alphabet
• From Greek: 'alpha' + 'beta' — the first two Greek letters
• 'Alphabetical list', 'in alphabetical order' — A to Z arrangement

quiz.question: The professor posed a hypothetical scenario to test the students' ethical reasoning. What does 'hypothetical' mean?
options: Supposed rather than actual — based on an imagined situation
Designed to reveal how students would respond to a real dilemma
Complex enough to require the application of ethical principles
Derived from actual cases in the professional literature
quiz.answer: Supposed rather than actual — based on an imagined situation
quiz.explanation:
Hypothetical means supposed — based on a hypothesis rather than reality.
• hypothetical = based on a hypothesis; not necessarily real
• From Greek: 'hypothesis' — a basis for reasoning
• 'Hypothetical scenario', 'a purely hypothetical' — imagined, not actual

quiz.question: His heretical view that the company should shrink to survive was unwelcome but prescient. What does 'heretical' mean?
options: Contrary to accepted doctrine — challenging orthodox belief
Radical enough to mark him as someone to be treated cautiously
Unpopular because it contradicted what everyone else was saying
Prescient in a way that improved his reputation in retrospect
quiz.answer: Contrary to accepted doctrine — challenging orthodox belief
quiz.explanation:
Heretical means departing from accepted doctrine — unorthodox and provocative.
• heretical = contrary to orthodox or accepted opinion
• From Greek: 'hairetikos' — able to choose
• 'Heretical idea', 'heretical in the church' — going against received wisdom

quiz.question: The research was conducted within a clear theoretical framework drawn from previous scholarship. What does 'theoretical' mean?
options: Concerned with theory rather than practical application
Based on published work rather than original observation
Establishing the conceptual foundation for the study
Drawing on existing models to guide the research design
quiz.answer: Concerned with theory rather than practical application
quiz.explanation:
Theoretical means relating to theory — concerned with ideas rather than practice.
• theoretical = concerned with theory; not practical
• From Greek: 'theoria' — looking at
• 'Theoretical physics', 'in theory' — the conceptual rather than the practical

quiz.question: The patient was admitted in a critical condition and spent three days in intensive care. What does 'critical' mean here?
options: Extremely serious — at a turning point between life and death
Severe enough to require immediate specialist intervention
Too unstable to be moved to a standard ward
Requiring monitoring at a level only intensive care could provide
quiz.answer: Extremely serious — at a turning point between life and death
quiz.explanation:
Critical (medically) means at a crisis point — extremely serious.
• critical = at a turning point; very serious; involving judgment
• From Greek: 'kritikos' — able to discern
• 'Critical condition', 'critical moment' — the decisive, dangerous point

quiz.question: She took a sceptical view of the claims, demanding evidence before she would be convinced. What does 'sceptical' mean?
options: Inclined to doubt — not easily persuaded without evidence
Critical of new ideas in a way that slowed their adoption
Trained to distinguish between good and poor evidence
Aware from experience that many claims did not withstand scrutiny
quiz.answer: Inclined to doubt — not easily persuaded without evidence
quiz.explanation:
Sceptical means inclined to doubt — questioning claims that lack sufficient evidence.
• sceptical = not easily convinced; inclined to question
• From Greek: 'skeptikos' — inquiring
• 'Sceptical audience', 'healthy scepticism' — demanding evidence

quiz.question: Planets travel in elliptical orbits around the Sun. What does 'elliptical' mean?
options: Having the shape of an ellipse — oval rather than circular
Slightly flattened in a way that distinguishes it from a circle
Following a path that brings the planet closer to the Sun at one point
Traced by a body moving under the influence of gravitational force
quiz.answer: Having the shape of an ellipse — oval rather than circular
quiz.explanation:
Elliptical means shaped like an ellipse — elongated and oval.
• elliptical = shaped like an ellipse; also: using fewer words than normal
• From Greek: 'elleipsis' — a falling short
• 'Elliptical orbit', 'elliptical speech' — oval shape or compressed expression

quiz.question: On this graph, the vertical axis represents the rate of growth over time. What does 'vertical' mean?
options: Running straight up and down — at right angles to the horizontal
Indicating the dependent variable in the chart's structure, as the evidence showed
Scaled to show the range of values being measured
Labelled to allow readers to identify specific data points
quiz.answer: Running straight up and down — at right angles to the horizontal
quiz.explanation:
Vertical means straight up and down — perpendicular to the horizon.
• vertical = at right angles to the horizontal; upright
• From Latin: 'vertex' — the highest point
• 'Vertical axis', 'vertical drop' — going straight up or down

quiz.question: The captain plotted the course on a nautical chart before leaving port. What does 'nautical' mean?
options: Relating to sailors, ships, and navigation at sea
Required for any voyage through unfamiliar waters
Produced by hydrographic agencies for professional mariners
Showing water depths, hazards, and navigation channels
quiz.answer: Relating to sailors, ships, and navigation at sea
quiz.explanation:
Nautical means relating to the sea and seafaring.
• nautical = relating to sailors, ships, or navigation
• From Greek: 'nautikos' — of sailors
• 'Nautical mile', 'nautical chart', 'nautical terms' — of the sea

quiz.question: The pharmaceutical company spent a decade developing the new drug. What does 'pharmaceutical' mean?
options: Relating to the preparation and use of medicinal drugs
Involved in research into treatments for serious diseases
Subject to regulatory approval before its products can be sold
Required to demonstrate safety and efficacy in clinical trials
quiz.answer: Relating to the preparation and use of medicinal drugs
quiz.explanation:
Pharmaceutical means relating to pharmacy — the preparation of medicines.
• pharmaceutical = relating to medicinal drugs
• From Greek: 'pharmakon' — a drug
• 'Pharmaceutical industry', 'pharmaceutical research' — drug development

quiz.question: Each vocabulary word is treated as a distinct lexical item in the database. What does 'lexical' mean?
options: Relating to the words of a language
Categorised by part of speech and frequency of use
Included in the corpus because it met the selection criteria
Listed with its definition, etymology, and example sentences
quiz.answer: Relating to the words of a language
quiz.explanation:
Lexical means relating to vocabulary — the words of a language.
• lexical = relating to the words of a language
• From Greek: 'lexikon' — of words
• 'Lexical item', 'lexical analysis', 'lexicon' — relating to words

quiz.question: The partnership was built on reciprocal respect — each party valued what the other brought. What does 'reciprocal' mean?
options: Mutual — given and received equally by both parties
Genuine enough to survive the pressures of a long partnership
Based on shared values rather than merely shared interests
Developed over time as each came to understand the other better
quiz.answer: Mutual — given and received equally by both parties
quiz.explanation:
Reciprocal means mutual — given and received in equal measure.
• reciprocal = given and received equally; mutual
• From Latin: 'reciprocus' — moving back and forth
• 'Reciprocal agreement', 'reciprocal trade' — equal exchange

quiz.question: The legal definition aimed to make the term univocal — meaning only one thing in all contexts. What does 'univocal' mean?
options: Having only one meaning — unambiguous
Defined with enough precision to resist misinterpretation
Consistent enough in its usage to have entered common use
Clear to all readers regardless of their legal background
quiz.answer: Having only one meaning — unambiguous
quiz.explanation:
Univocal means having a single clear meaning — unambiguous.
• univocal = having only one meaning; unambiguous
• From Latin: 'uni-' (one) + 'vox' (voice)
• Opposite of equivocal; 'a univocal term' = means exactly one thing

quiz.question: She won the gold medal in the 400 metres at the world championships. What is a medal?
options: A metal disc awarded as a prize or honour
The highest award available in an athletic competition
A prize that recognised her as the best in the world
A trophy presented at the ceremony following the final
quiz.answer: A metal disc awarded as a prize or honour
quiz.explanation:
A medal is a metal disc — awarded to recognise achievement or merit.
• medal = a metal disc awarded for achievement or service
• From French: 'médaille'
• 'Gold medal', 'military medal' — awarded for achievement or bravery

quiz.question: The campaign promoted pedal power as an alternative to car travel. What does 'pedal power' mean?
options: The use of cycling as a form of human-powered transport
An environmental campaign targeting urban commuters
A movement encouraging people to leave their cars at home
A scheme that provided incentives for cycling to work
quiz.answer: The use of cycling as a form of human-powered transport
quiz.explanation:
Pedal power refers to cycling — using human leg effort to power a bicycle.
• pedal = a lever worked by the foot
• From Latin: 'pedalis' — of the foot
• 'Pedal power', 'pedal cycle', 'put the pedal down' — foot-powered

quiz.question: She wore leather sandals on the beach that left marks on the sand. What is a sandal?
options: An open shoe held to the foot by straps
A lightweight footwear choice suited to warm weather
A shoe designed to allow air to reach the foot
Traditional footwear worn in hot climates for thousands of years
quiz.answer: An open shoe held to the foot by straps
quiz.explanation:
A sandal is an open shoe — consisting of a sole held to the foot by straps.
• sandal = an open shoe consisting of a sole with straps
• From Greek: 'sandalion'
• 'Leather sandal', 'Jesus sandals', 'platform sandal' — open footwear

quiz.question: Time does not always heal old wounds. What does 'heal' mean?
options: To restore to health — to make well again
To reduce the pain of something that has caused lasting damage
To lessen the emotional impact of a difficult experience
To put behind one something that had been very painful
quiz.answer: To restore to health — to make well again
quiz.explanation:
To heal means to restore to health — to recover from injury or pain.
• heal = to restore to health; to repair
• From Old English: 'hælan' — to make whole
• 'Heal a wound', 'heal a rift' — restoring to wholeness

quiz.question: Critics argued that piecemeal reform would not solve the underlying problem. What does 'piecemeal' mean?
options: Done in small separate stages rather than all at once
Designed to address symptoms rather than causes
Inadequate to the scale of the problem it was meant to solve
Introduced without a coherent plan for what would follow
quiz.answer: Done in small separate stages rather than all at once
quiz.explanation:
Piecemeal means done gradually in parts — without a comprehensive plan.
• piecemeal = done gradually in parts; not comprehensive
• From 'piece' + 'meal' (Old English: 'mæl' — a portion)
• 'Piecemeal approach', 'piecemeal changes' — bit by bit rather than wholesale

quiz.question: She started every winter morning with a bowl of oatmeal porridge. What is oatmeal?
options: Ground oats used for porridge and baking
A grain product used as the base for a traditional breakfast dish
A processed cereal that requires cooking before eating
A high-fibre food associated with a healthy morning routine
quiz.answer: Ground oats used for porridge and baking
quiz.explanation:
Oatmeal is ground oats — used to make porridge or in baking.
• oatmeal = meal made from ground oats
• From 'oat' + 'meal' (ground grain)
• 'Oatmeal porridge', 'oatmeal cookie' — made from ground oats

quiz.question: She was a lineal descendant of the original founder of the company. What does 'lineal' mean?
options: In a direct line of descent — through an unbroken parent-child line
Related through blood rather than through marriage
Able to trace her ancestry directly to the founder
Entitled to inherit because of a direct family connection, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: In a direct line of descent — through an unbroken parent-child line
quiz.explanation:
Lineal means in direct line of descent — through parent to child without interruption.
• lineal = in a direct line of ancestry or descent
• From Latin: 'linea' — a line
• 'Lineal descendant' = direct descendant; 'lateral' = through siblings

quiz.question: Parliament voted to repeal the outdated legislation. What does 'repeal' mean?
options: To revoke a law — to formally cancel it
To replace an old law with a new one that achieved the same purpose
To vote against a law that had been in force for many years
To remove a law from the statute book through parliamentary procedure
quiz.answer: To revoke a law — to formally cancel it
quiz.explanation:
To repeal means to revoke — to formally cancel a law through legislation.
• repeal = to revoke or annul a law by legislative act
• From Old French: 'rapeler' — to call back
• 'Repeal an act', 'repeal and replace' — cancelling legislation

quiz.question: She made an appeal against the original decision. What is an appeal?
options: A formal request for a decision to be reviewed by a higher authority
A legal challenge to a ruling made by a lower court
A process that allowed a judgment to be reconsidered
A mechanism for correcting errors made in earlier proceedings, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A formal request for a decision to be reviewed by a higher authority
quiz.explanation:
An appeal is a formal request for review — asking a higher authority to reconsider.
• appeal = a request for a decision to be reviewed; an attraction
• From Latin: 'appellare' — to call to
• 'Appeal a sentence', 'court of appeal' — seeking a review

quiz.question: He poured a bowl of breakfast cereal and added cold milk. What is cereal?
options: A grain-based food product typically eaten for breakfast
A processed food made from one or more types of grain
A food associated with a quick and nutritious morning meal
Any grain product that can be eaten with milk
quiz.answer: A grain-based food product typically eaten for breakfast
quiz.explanation:
Cereal is a grain-based food — from grain crops, typically eaten as breakfast.
• cereal = a grain crop; a food product made from grain
• From Latin: 'Ceres' — the goddess of agriculture
• 'Breakfast cereal', 'cereal crop' — grain or grain-based food

quiz.question: Astronomers use sidereal time rather than solar time to track star positions. What does 'sidereal' mean?
options: Relating to the stars — measured with respect to the fixed stars
More precise than the solar time used in everyday calendars
Preferred by astronomers because it does not drift over centuries
Based on the rotation of the Earth relative to distant stars
quiz.answer: Relating to the stars — measured with respect to the fixed stars
quiz.explanation:
Sidereal means relating to the stars — measured relative to the fixed stars.
• sidereal = of or relating to the stars
• From Latin: 'sidus' — a star
• 'Sidereal day', 'sidereal year' — measured relative to the fixed stars

quiz.question: She possessed an ethereal beauty that seemed to belong to another world. What does 'ethereal' mean?
options: Delicately light and otherworldly — too perfect for the material world
Striking in a way that was difficult to describe precisely
Distinct from conventional standards of attractiveness
Associated more with grace than with conventional good looks, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Delicately light and otherworldly — too perfect for the material world
quiz.explanation:
Ethereal means delicately otherworldly — too fine and light for the material world.
• ethereal = extremely delicate; heavenly
• From Greek: 'aither' — the upper air beyond the clouds
• 'Ethereal beauty', 'ethereal voice' — heavenly, unworldly delicacy

quiz.question: A funereal mood hung over the office after the announcement of the redundancies. What does 'funereal' mean?
options: Mournfully dark and gloomy — like a funeral
Quiet in a way that reflected the seriousness of the news
Subdued enough that even normally cheerful people were silent
Heavy with the knowledge that working life had changed permanently
quiz.answer: Mournfully dark and gloomy — like a funeral
quiz.explanation:
Funereal means deeply gloomy — resembling the atmosphere of a funeral.
• funereal = having the mournful character of a funeral
• From Latin: 'funereus' — of a funeral
• 'Funereal pace', 'funereal atmosphere' — dark and solemn as a funeral

quiz.question: The orangutan is an arboreal species that spends most of its life in trees. What does 'arboreal' mean?
options: Living in or relating to trees
Dependent on forest environments for food and shelter
Adapted physically to climbing and moving through canopy
Threatened by the loss of the forest habitat it requires
quiz.answer: Living in or relating to trees
quiz.explanation:
Arboreal means tree-dwelling — living in or adapted to life in trees.
• arboreal = relating to trees; living in trees
• From Latin: 'arbor' — a tree
• 'Arboreal animal', 'arboreal habitat' — tree-living

quiz.question: Ghost stories typically feature incorporeal spirits that cannot be touched. What does 'incorporeal' mean?
options: Without physical form — not consisting of matter
Invisible to ordinary observers under normal conditions
Present in a space without being detectable by normal means
Existing in a state between the living world and whatever lies beyond
quiz.answer: Without physical form — not consisting of matter
quiz.explanation:
Incorporeal means without a physical body — non-material.
• incorporeal = not composed of matter; without a physical form
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'corpus' (body)
• 'Incorporeal being', 'incorporeal rights' — non-physical

quiz.question: The artist sought to capture the empyreal light that filled the cathedral. What does 'empyreal' mean?
options: Belonging to the highest heaven — celestial and sublime
Beautiful in a way that suggested divine rather than earthly origin
Filtered through stained glass in a way unique to sacred spaces
So intense that it seemed to come from beyond the building itself
quiz.answer: Belonging to the highest heaven — celestial and sublime
quiz.explanation:
Empyreal means of the highest heaven — celestially pure and sublime.
• empyreal = of the empyrean; celestial; sublimely lofty
• From Greek: 'empyros' — fiery
• 'Empyreal light', 'empyrean' — the highest heaven, realm of pure fire or light

quiz.question: She entered the room with a regal bearing that silenced everyone. What does 'regal' mean?
options: Of or befitting a monarch — stately and commanding
More imposing than one would expect from someone of her age
Formal in a way that commanded instant respect
Confident enough to change the atmosphere of a room
quiz.answer: Of or befitting a monarch — stately and commanding
quiz.explanation:
Regal means royal — having or displaying the dignity of a monarch.
• regal = of or befitting a monarch; stately
• From Latin: 'regalis' — of a king
• 'Regal bearing', 'regal manner' — kingly or queenly in quality

quiz.question: The prodigal son returned after squandering his inheritance and was welcomed back. What does 'prodigal' mean?
options: Wasteful and recklessly extravagant
Lost for so long that his return was unexpected
Repentant enough to deserve the welcome he received
Young enough to learn from the mistakes he had made
quiz.answer: Wasteful and recklessly extravagant
quiz.explanation:
Prodigal means recklessly wasteful — spending with extravagant abandon.
• prodigal = recklessly extravagant; wasteful
• From Latin: 'prodigere' — to squander
• 'Prodigal son' (Luke 15), 'prodigal spending' — waste and extravagance

quiz.question: The group performed madrigals from the Elizabethan period. What is a madrigal?
options: A part-song for several voices — typically on an amorous theme
A Renaissance vocal form performed without instrumental accompaniment
A type of song that flourished in England in the late 16th century
A choral form that used counterpoint to weave multiple melodic lines
quiz.answer: A part-song for several voices — typically on an amorous theme
quiz.explanation:
A madrigal is a secular vocal composition — typically for several unaccompanied voices.
• madrigal = a part-song for several voices, typically amorous
• From Italian: 'madrigale'
• 'Elizabethan madrigal', 'sing a madrigal' — Renaissance secular vocal music

quiz.question: The centrifugal force sent the car skidding to the outside of the bend. What is centrifugal force?
options: The apparent force that pushes objects outward in a rotating system
The force that prevents a vehicle from following a curved path
A physical phenomenon produced by turning at high speed
The tendency of matter to move away from the centre of rotation, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The apparent force that pushes objects outward in a rotating system
quiz.explanation:
Centrifugal force is the apparent outward force in rotation — pushing away from the centre.
• centrifugal = moving or tending to move away from the centre
• From Latin: 'centrum' (centre) + 'fugere' (to flee)
• 'Centrifugal force', 'centrifuge' — moving away from the centre

quiz.question: The law recognised the conjugal rights of both spouses. What does 'conjugal' mean?
options: Relating to marriage — of the married relationship
Belonging to both parties in a legal union
Recognised by law as arising from the state of matrimony
Part of the mutual obligations that marriage creates
quiz.answer: Relating to marriage — of the married relationship
quiz.explanation:
Conjugal means relating to marriage — of or pertaining to the married state.
• conjugal = relating to marriage or the relationship between spouses
• From Latin: 'conjugalis' — relating to a yoke (joining)
• 'Conjugal rights', 'conjugal visit' — pertaining to a married couple

quiz.question: The story about his wild youth is probably apocryphal — nobody can confirm it. What does 'apocryphal' mean?
options: Of doubtful authenticity — widely told but probably untrue
Interesting enough to have survived without anyone checking it
Entertaining but lacking the documentation that would verify it
Told so often that it had acquired a life of its own
quiz.answer: Of doubtful authenticity — widely told but probably untrue
quiz.explanation:
Apocryphal means of doubtful authenticity — widely circulated but unverifiable.
• apocryphal = of doubtful authenticity; widely told but probably untrue
• From Greek: 'apokryphos' — hidden away
• 'Apocryphal story', 'apocryphal saying' — good story, probably not true

quiz.question: He was promoted to field marshal after leading the decisive campaign. What is a field marshal?
options: The highest rank in many armies — a senior general
An officer appointed to command a major theatre of war
A rank achieved only by those with an outstanding service record
A general whose promotion was approved by the head of state
quiz.answer: The highest rank in many armies — a senior general
quiz.explanation:
A field marshal is the highest military rank — the most senior general.
• field marshal = the highest rank of general officer in many armies
• From Old French: 'mareschal' — one who cares for horses
• 'Promoted to field marshal', 'Field Marshal Montgomery' — the top rank

quiz.question: The couple settled into connubial bliss after years of friendship. What does 'connubial' mean?
options: Relating to marriage — of the married state
Characteristic of a happy and well-matched partnership
Achieved only after a long period of knowing each other
Associated with domestic life and shared routines
quiz.answer: Relating to marriage — of the married state
quiz.explanation:
Connubial means of or relating to marriage — the state of being married.
• connubial = relating to marriage or the relationship of spouses
• From Latin: 'connubium' — marriage
• 'Connubial bliss', 'connubial harmony' — the state of matrimony

quiz.question: Progress on the legislation moved at a glacial pace. What does 'glacial' mean?
options: Extremely slow — moving as imperceptibly as a glacier
Deliberate in a way that frustrated those waiting for action
Slowed by the number of parties whose agreement was required
Proceeding carefully to avoid errors that could be exploited
quiz.answer: Extremely slow — moving as imperceptibly as a glacier
quiz.explanation:
Glacial means extremely slow — like the imperceptible movement of a glacier.
• glacial = of or relating to glaciers; extremely slow
• From Latin: 'glacies' — ice
• 'Glacial pace', 'glacial stare' — either very slow or very cold

quiz.question: The decision was challenged through judicial review in the High Court. What is judicial review?
options: A court's examination of whether a public body acted lawfully
An appeal brought against a decision that was believed to be wrong
A process for overturning a government decision in court
A mechanism for challenging administrative decisions
quiz.answer: A court's examination of whether a public body acted lawfully
quiz.explanation:
Judicial review is a court's examination of whether public decisions were lawful.
• judicial review = court examination of the lawfulness of public body decisions
• From Latin: 'judicialis' — of a court
• 'Apply for judicial review', 'subject to judicial review' — legal scrutiny

quiz.question: Despite their political differences, the two leaders maintained cordial relations. What does 'cordial' mean?
options: Warm and friendly — showing sincere goodwill
Professional enough that personal differences did not interfere
Respectful in a way that made diplomacy easier
More positive than the political circumstances might have produced
quiz.answer: Warm and friendly — showing sincere goodwill
quiz.explanation:
Cordial means warm and friendly — showing sincere goodwill.
• cordial = warm and friendly; a sweet fruit drink
• From Latin: 'cor' — the heart
• 'Cordial welcome', 'cordial relations' — from the heart, warm

quiz.question: Humans have a vestigial tail — a small bone called the coccyx that is all that remains. What does 'vestigial' mean?
options: Present as a remnant of something that was once more developed
Too small to be visible without specialist imaging
Retained because it still played a minor anatomical role
A survival from an earlier evolutionary stage of development, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Present as a remnant of something that was once more developed
quiz.explanation:
Vestigial means surviving as a trace — a remnant of something once larger or functional.
• vestigial = forming a small remnant of something that was once greater
• From Latin: 'vestigium' — a footprint, trace
• 'Vestigial tail', 'vestigial organ' — a remnant of an evolutionary ancestor

quiz.question: His parochial view of the world prevented him from understanding how others lived. What does 'parochial' mean?
options: Narrow in perspective — concerned only with one's own locality
Resistant to ideas that came from outside his immediate experience
Unable to imagine conditions different from those he had grown up in
Formed by an education that had not exposed him to wider perspectives
quiz.answer: Narrow in perspective — concerned only with one's own locality
quiz.explanation:
Parochial means narrow-minded — limited to local concerns and perspectives.
• parochial = relating to a parish; having a narrow outlook
• From Latin: 'parochia' — a parish
• 'Parochial view', 'parochial attitude' — limited to local concerns

quiz.question: She felt a strong sense of filial duty that kept her close to her ageing parents. What does 'filial' mean?
options: Relating to the relationship of a child to a parent
Felt by children toward parents in many cultures
Expressed through practical care as well as emotional closeness
More strongly felt in some families than in others
quiz.answer: Relating to the relationship of a child to a parent
quiz.explanation:
Filial means of a child toward a parent — pertaining to the son or daughter relationship.
• filial = relating to a son or daughter
• From Latin: 'filius' (son) / 'filia' (daughter)
• 'Filial duty', 'filial piety' — the obligations of a child toward parents

quiz.question: The party's success owed much to their genial host. What does 'genial' mean?
options: Friendly and cheerful — warmly good-natured
Experienced enough to make any gathering run smoothly
Well-liked enough that guests always accepted his invitations
Attentive to guests in a way that made everyone feel welcome
quiz.answer: Friendly and cheerful — warmly good-natured
quiz.explanation:
Genial means warmly friendly — pleasantly cheerful and good-natured.
• genial = friendly and cheerful; pleasantly warm
• From Latin: 'genius' — spirit of festivity
• 'A genial host', 'genial atmosphere' — warm, friendly, convivial

quiz.question: She found the new colleagues congenial — they shared her values and her sense of humour. What does 'congenial' mean?
options: Pleasant and agreeable — suited to one's own tastes
Similar enough in outlook to make working together easy
Professional in a way that allowed friendships to develop naturally
Able to create an atmosphere that brought out the best in everyone
quiz.answer: Pleasant and agreeable — suited to one's own tastes
quiz.explanation:
Congenial means agreeable and pleasant — matching one's tastes and temperament.
• congenial = pleasant because suited to one's character
• From Latin: 'con-' + 'genius' (spirit, nature)
• 'Congenial company', 'congenial work' — matching in spirit and temperament
